justification: All rubric criteria met: both training (weekly batch) and serving (Prediction API with in-process model) pipelines fully designed with clear boundaries. 100ms P99 constraint explicitly addressed with latency budget breakdown (5ms network + 10ms feature fetch + 5ms inference + 2ms serialization + 78ms headroom). Feature store identified as necessary with dual online/offline design and marked as a rigidity point. Model versioning with immutable artifacts and blue-green/canary deployment with auto-rollback designed via Deployment Controller. Team size factored throughout — explicitly rejects Kubernetes, Kubeflow, TF Serving, Triton, and GPU infrastructure for a 4-person team; recommends managed services and simple tooling. Trade-offs and risks are extensive and well-reasoned.

observations.text: Exceptional latency budget breakdown showing exactly how 100ms is allocated across the serving path
Training-serving skew identified as critical risk with Feature Store as shared kernel — shows deep ML systems understanding
Practical model choice (LightGBM/XGBoost) justified against deep learning and simpler alternatives, with sub-5ms inference
Phased implementation plan (4 phases over 8+ weeks) is realistic for a 4-person team
Graceful degradation designed — fallback predictions with wider confidence interval if Redis or model fails
Team ownership split (2 ML + 2 platform) maps cleanly to component boundaries
Feature freshness SLAs per feature type with monitoring and alerting
In-process model loading justified over dedicated model server, saving a network hop and operational complexity

agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "A lightweight ML serving architecture for real-time delivery time prediction, built around a pre-computed feature store, a versioned model registry with blue-green deployment, and an offline training pipeline. The design prioritizes sub-100ms P99 latency at the serving layer, safe weekly model rollouts with instant rollback, and a clean separation between the training and serving bounded contexts. Sized for a 4-person team — no Kubernetes, no feature platform, no overen
